one_line_takeaway: For concept erasure in Stable Diffusion, adaptively preserving the concept most sensitive to the parameter update (found by a Gumbel-Softmax min-max search) beats preserving a fixed neutral anchor, roughly doubling preservation of unrelated concepts while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art erasure.
key_finding: Across all three settings the method achieves the best erasure-vs-preservation balance. It is the empirical demonstration (Figures 1-3) that neutral concepts sit in the MIDDLE of the sensitivity spectrum while related concepts (e.g., 'person', 'women' for target 'nudity') are far more sensitive, that motivates and validates preserving adaptively-chosen sensitive concepts over a fixed neutral anchor. Quantitatively, on Imagenette object erasure it nearly matches top erasure while roughly doubling the best baseline's preservation (PSR-1 55.2% vs CA 44.2%, vs UCE which collapses to 23.4% despite perfect 100% erasure). On nudity it gives the lowest NER at every threshold (3.64% at 0.3 vs ESD 5.32%, UCE 6.87%, CA 13.84%, SD 16.7%) AND the best FID (15.52). On artistic style it gives the strongest erasure (to-erase CLIP 21.57 vs ESD 23.56) with comparable retention.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Adversarial Concept Preservation, a fine-tuning method for erasing undesirable concepts from text-to-image diffusion models (Stable Diffusion) while minimizing collateral damage to unrelated concepts. Its OWN contribution is NOT the erasure objective (L1, forcing the target concept's noise prediction toward a neutral/null concept, is the pre-existing 'naive' approach used by ESD/TIME/UCE). The contribution is the preservation term L2: instead of preserving a fixed neutral anchor like 'a photo' or the empty string as prior work does, the method adaptively identifies, at each step, the 'adversarial concept' ca in the remaining concept set that is MOST sensitive to the current parameter change, and explicitly preserves it. This is cast as a bilevel min-max optimization (outer min erases + preserves; inner max searches for the most-affected concept). Because concepts are discrete, they relax the search to a distribution over the concept-embedding simplex and use the Gumbel-Softmax trick to keep it differentiable, which they show yields meaningful related concepts rather than the background/color collapse that plain PGD in continuous embedding space produces.
threat_model: Defensive / content-safety framing. The risk being mitigated is that text-to-image diffusion models trained on unfiltered internet data can be prompted to generate harmful or undesirable content (NSFW/nudity, violence, racism) and can mimic copyrighted artistic styles. The paper positions concept erasure via model fine-tuning as the most practical sanitization strategy for open-source models (versus training-data pre-filtering, which requires full retraining, or post-hoc NSFW output filters, which are easily bypassed or disabled by editing a few lines of code). The specific problem it solves is the utility/safety trade-off: naive erasure removes the bad concept but degrades the model's ability to generate legitimately-related concepts (e.g., erasing 'nudity' harms 'woman'/'person'). Note: robustness against adversarial jailbreak prompts is NOT this paper's focus (that is the concurrent AdvUnlearn work it distinguishes itself from); its 'adversarial' search targets parameter-sensitivity for preservation, not attack resistance.
limitations: (1) Preservation is improved but far from solved: even the proposed method retains only 55.2% PSR-1 / 79.9% PSR-5 versus the original SD's 78.0% / 97.6%, so multi-class object erasure still meaningfully degrades unrelated generation. (2) On pure erasure it is not the leader in every setting: UCE reaches 100% object ESR (method gets 98.6%/96.1%), so the gain is specifically in the preservation dimension, not raw removal. (3) Computational cost scales with the concept-space size because Gumbel-Softmax requires embedding the whole search space; the authors must restrict to a K-nearest subset, and nudity erasure (all non-cross-attention modules) costs ~6h vs ~1h for other concepts. (4) The naive continuous PGD search collapses to background/color concepts, requiring the Gumbel-Softmax discretization workaround. (5) Hyperparameter sensitivity: reducing K from 100 to 20 drops ESR/PSR by several points, and raising Niter from 2 to 8 hurts PSR-1 by ~10%. (6) Adversarial-jailbreak robustness of the sanitized model is not evaluated.

datasets_used: Imagenette (10-class ImageNet subset; erase 5 classes at once, 500 images/class, ResNet-50 detector)
I2P prompts (4703 generated NSFW images; NudeNet/Praneet-2019 detector)
COCO-30K (FID preservation evaluation for nudity setting)
Artistic-style prompts for 4 artists: Kelly McKernan, Thomas Kinkade, Tyler Edlin, Kilian Eng (200 images/artist)
Oxford-3000 word list as adversarial-concept search space (alt: CLIP token vocabulary, 49,408 tokens)
quantitative_results: Object erasure (Table 1), Ours: ESR-1 98.6%, ESR-5 96.1%, PSR-1 55.2%, PSR-5 79.9%
Object erasure baselines: UCE ESR 100%/100% but PSR-1 23.4%/PSR-5 49.5%; CA ESR-1 98.4%/PSR-1 44.2%/PSR-5 66.5%; ESD ESR-1 95.5%/PSR-1 41.2%; original SD PSR-1 78.0%/PSR-5 97.6%
Nudity NER at threshold 0.3 (Table 2, lower better): Ours 3.64%, ESD 5.32%, UCE 6.87%, CA 13.84%, original SD 16.7%
Nudity FID on COCO-30K (Table 2, lower better): Ours 15.52 (best), UCE 15.98, ESD 17.14, CA 20.76
Nudity NER at threshold 0.8: Ours 0.06% vs ESD 0.23%, UCE 0.21%, CA 1.68%
Artistic style (Table 4), to-erase CLIP (lower=better erasure): Ours 21.57, ESD 23.56, UCE 24.47, CA 27.79; to-retain CLIP: Ours 30.13 (comparable to baselines' ~29.6-30.9)
baselines_compared: UCE - Unified Concept Editing (Gandikota et al., 2024)
